questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Week 1 review?

options.text: To capture baseline metrics and identify system friction points
To decide whether to quit the sprint

questions.explanation: Week 1 review has two jobs: capture baseline metrics (your Week 1 numbers become the reference point for Weeks 2-4) and identify friction points (what broke or slowed you down). These become the data for Week 2 adjustments.

questions.question: Week 1 is almost never perfect. According to the lesson, what should you treat imperfections as?
questions.explanation: Systems break, automations fail, sequences underperform, calls run over, life interrupts. None of that is failure — it is data. The review session is where you learn faster than people who just execute without looking back.

questions.question: What is the most valuable part of the Week 1 review?
questions.explanation: Identifying friction clearly is the most valuable part. Something broke or slowed you down — pinpointing exactly what it was (infrastructure friction, execution friction, or quality friction) enables targeted fixes in Week 2.

questions.question: If your planned email volume in Week 1 was 120 but you only sent 87, the sprint has already failed.
questions.explanation: False. Falling short of planned volume in Week 1 is normal and expected. The important thing is to understand why (deliverability issues, time management, prospect research taking longer) and fix the specific friction point for Week 2.

questions.question: If you were conducting a Week 1 review, what planned vs. actual metrics would you track? What friction points would you anticipate in your first week of execution?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response identifies specific planned vs. actual metrics (emails, calls, hours), anticipates realistic friction points (deliverability, time management, tool configuration), and proposes a fix approach for the most likely friction.
